claims.claim_text: The opening release-blocking incident is presented as a concrete real event with specific operational details but has no attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: At midnight before a holiday sale, a platform team member receives a page from the release channel rather than the monitoring system. The deployment is blocked, not because the application tests failed, but because the container scan found a critical remote-code-execution vulnerability in the base image used by every checkout service. The first reaction in the room is frustration: the feature is ready, the business deadline is real, and nobody wants a security tool deciding whether revenue ships.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A release can be blocked not because the application tests failed, but because a container scan finds a critical remote-code-execution vulnerability in a base image used by a business-critical service. The first reaction is often frustration: the feature is ready, the deadline is real, and nobody wants a security tool deciding whether a release can ship.
claims.rationale: This reads like a sourced incident narrative but provides no named organization, postmortem, or primary reference for the specific scenario.

claims.claim_text: The follow-up paragraph continues the same unsourced incident with precise exploitability and exposure details as if it were a documented real event.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Five minutes later, the room changes tone. The affected package is present in the runtime layer, the service is internet-facing, and the exploit requires only crafted input that the checkout service already accepts. The pipeline did not create the risk; it exposed a risk that was already riding toward production inside a green build. The gate forced the organization to make an explicit decision while there was still time to fix the image, route traffic around the affected service, or accept the risk with named ownership.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The tone changes once the team confirms that the vulnerable package is in the runtime layer, the service is exposed to real traffic, and the exploit path is relevant to how the service is used. The pipeline did not create the risk; it exposed a risk that was already headed toward production inside a green build. The gate forced the organization to make an explicit decision while there was still time to fix the image, route traffic around the affected service, or accept the risk with named ownership.
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the paragraph presents incident-specific facts without any attributable incident source.

claims.claim_text: Image digests are immutable content hashes, while tags can move, so digest-based references remove tag-time ambiguity.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | artifact immutability paragraph
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes images documentation directly states that digests are immutable hashes of image content and that tags can be moved.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions supports job-specific `GITHUB_TOKEN` permissions, so different jobs can run with different least-privilege scopes.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | least privilege per job paragraph
claims.rationale: GitHub's workflow syntax reference explicitly documents `jobs.<job_id>.permissions` and per-job permission scoping, but `docs.github.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` action uploads SARIF to GitHub code scanning, and advanced setup workflows need `security-events: write` permission.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | Minimal Permission Boundary workflow and later SARIF examples
claims.rationale: The upstream CodeQL Action repository directly documents `upload-sarif` and the required `security-events: write` permission.

claims.claim_text: GitHub Actions jobs must grant `id-token:[REDACTED_SECRET] to request an OIDC token.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | Minimal Permission Boundary workflow
claims.rationale: GitHub's OIDC reference explicitly says the job or workflow must grant `id-token:[REDACTED_SECRET], but `docs.github.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Third-party actions and similar pipeline components execute code in the delivery path and a compromised action can expose secrets or abuse the workflow token.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | controlled dependency on third-party pipeline components paragraph
claims.rationale: GitHub's secure-use reference explicitly warns that third-party actions are a significant risk and can access repository secrets and the workflow token, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Pinning a GitHub Action to a full-length commit SHA is the only way to treat that action reference as immutable.
claims.span_hint: section: Threat Modeling the Pipeline Itself | paragraph after Minimal Permission Boundary workflow
claims.rationale: GitHub's secure-use guidance states that pinning to a full-length commit SHA is currently the only immutable action reference, but `docs.github.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The Trivy GitHub Action supports CI scanning of filesystems, images, and configuration and can emit SARIF output.
claims.span_hint: section: Static Analysis, Dependency Scanning, and Secrets; section: Container Image and IaC Scanning
claims.rationale: The upstream Trivy Action README documents `fs`, `image`, and config-oriented scan modes plus SARIF output examples.

claims.claim_text: Publicly exposed secrets can be found quickly by automated scanners, so secret scanning belongs early in the pipeline.
claims.span_hint: section: Choosing the Right Security Checks | secrets scanning paragraph
claims.rationale: GitHub's leaked-secret remediation guide explicitly says automated scanners can locate publicly exposed secrets in minutes, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A safer pod baseline uses non-root execution, disables privilege escalation, can set a read-only root filesystem, and drops Linux capabilities.
claims.span_hint: section: Container Image and IaC Scanning | safer-example explanation
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes security-context task directly documents these hardening controls and what each field does.

claims.claim_text: GitHub-hosted runners provision a new VM for each job, so local Docker images are not shared across separate jobs.
claims.span_hint: section: Container Image and IaC Scanning | paragraph after the build-and-scan/push workflow
claims.rationale: GitHub's hosted-runner docs say GitHub provisions a new VM for each job, but `docs.github.com` is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: SARIF is a standardized interchange format for static-analysis results.
claims.rationale: OASIS is the standards body for SARIF and its announcement page directly identifies SARIF v2.1.0 as an OASIS Standard.

claims.claim_text: Secret remediation requires revocation or rotation; removing the secret from the codebase alone does not make it safe again.
claims.span_hint: section: Static Analysis, Dependency Scanning, and Secrets | secret scanning remediation paragraph; section: Did You Know? | item 4
claims.rationale: GitHub's remediation guide explicitly says simply removing a leaked secret from the repository does not prevent exploitation and advises revocation, but the host is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The release-window war story is presented as a specific organizational event but has no attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Pipeline That Saved a Release Window | paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: An e-commerce company planned a major checkout release shortly before its busiest sales weekend. The platform had strong unit tests and good deployment automation, but security checks were uneven. Some services ran SAST, a few teams used dependency scanning, and most container images were pushed to the registry without a blocking image scan. The organization believed it had "shifted left" because developers saw some warnings in pull requests, but the final runtime artifacts were not consistently inspected.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A delivery organization can reach a high-pressure release window with good tests and deployment automation but uneven security checks. Teams may see some warnings in pull requests and believe they have shifted left, while final runtime artifacts still are not consistently inspected.
claims.rationale: This is framed like a real company incident without any postmortem, company name, or other attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The next paragraph of the release-window story adds date-like timing and concrete findings without an attributable incident source.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Pipeline That Saved a Release Window | paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: Two days before the release freeze, the platform team added a container scan to the shared build template. The first run was painful. Multiple services carried critical vulnerabilities in base-image packages, several images ran as root, and some services included build tools that were never needed at runtime. The findings were not new risks introduced that day; they were accumulated risk made visible by a stronger checkpoint.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When a team adds container scanning to a shared build template, the first run can expose accumulated risk such as vulnerable base-image packages, root-running images, and unnecessary runtime tools. The findings are often not new risks introduced that day; they are existing risk made visible by a stronger checkpoint.
claims.rationale: The paragraph reads as a specific release incident, but no cited postmortem or primary source backs the concrete scenario.

claims.claim_text: SBOMs improve the speed with which affected components and vulnerabilities can be identified and remediated.
claims.rationale: NIST's SBOM guidance explicitly says SBOMs increase the speed at which vulnerabilities can be identified and remediated.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Images
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for image tags, digests, immutability, and why digest-based promotion matters.

further_reading.title: Configure a Security Context for a Pod or Container
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary reference for non-root execution, dropped capabilities, read-only filesystems, and privilege controls used in safer manifests.

further_reading.title: Trivy GitHub Action
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows the exact CI integration patterns the module uses for filesystem, image, and configuration scanning.

further_reading.title: GitHub CodeQL Action
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers SARIF upload, code-scanning integration, and workflow permissions used in the GitHub Actions examples.

further_reading.title: NIST: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides authoritative background on SBOM purpose, supply-chain visibility, and vulnerability-response value.

further_reading.title: OASIS SARIF Standard Announcement
further_reading.why_relevant: Confirms SARIF as the governing standard behind the code-scanning interchange format discussed in the module.

notes: Skipped quizzes, exercises, ASCII diagrams, and code blocks. I also skipped most qualitative tool-comparison rows unless the prose made a load-bearing vendor or standards claim that directly affects the module's CI/CD examples or security decisions.
